item.content:encoded:
The article is as big as it needs to be, and as stated above, does leave a lot to the imagination.
Also a bit refreshing to see a shorter article nowadays. Good job on the writing!

Left me thinking "What if we all have cats on our heads?"

Interesting…

I suppose this answers the age-old question of "did we domesticate cats or did cats domesticate us?"
I for one welcome our feline overlords.

item.title: Re: To Put a Cat on Your Head
item.content:encoded:
Here is the image source, just for reference.

Source: Wikimedia Commons
License: CC0

Title: File:猫図-Cat Seen from Behind MET DP211876.jpg
Author: Kawabata Gyokusho (川端玉章)
Date: 1868

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
// SCP-5610 is an anomalous entity that is only observable by individuals with a cognitive resistance value of 5.6.//

At least 5.6? Under 5.7? Exactly 5.6?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cute idea and as long enough as it needs to be.

There are two things which I don't think worked well and stopped me from upvoting, though. I don't like the use of the cognitive resistance value here - it doesn't fit with what I think the CRV should be, which is basically worldbuilding fluff. At most, it only exists to be a sciency-sounding scale for "people who go crazy towards cognitohazards vs people who aren't affected" as applied to a document about the supernatural. A specific value (not less than, greater than, or a range) implies that it has some kind of inherent meaning, which gels badly with something which is much older than the Foundation and modern civilisation. I don't think an additional nod to the number 56 (the reference of which I didn't get, though that's not necessarily a fault of the SCP) is worth introducing this confusion.

I'm also wary of the phrase "as a subject's behavior in public becomes more congruent to their behavior in private, the instance of SCP-5610 becomes increasingly detailed". Readers might be able to imagine what this means, but it's pretty vague. I appreciate the aim not to introduce additional details in an article like this, but the question of "how would the Foundation even have measured this" comes up, and I think that could have used another line or two of fleshing out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What a delightfully petite work. Definitely leaves a lot to the imagination while simultaneously being enjoyable enough on its own. Good work with this!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was really great!!! Good job! I would love to see some experiment logs, maybe something where the cat of a researcher manifested and they just watch it for some anomalous behavior? Maybe have the cat become a part of the site and maybe die or something? Idk…

Anyways, phenomenal work! I would love to see some experiment logs
+1

item.title: To Put a Cat on Your Head
item.content:encoded:
Heya, Eze here with a breather article about cats and haikus. I based this on the idiom 'Neko wo kaburu', which means to feign a personality, disguising your true one. It's also a part of Japanese culture, particularly with honmae and tatemae.

Special thanks to The Great Hippo, JakdragonX, and Noodles for looking over this, revising and strengthening it, and telling me what parts I should look for.

Image credits: "Cat Seen from Behind" by Kawabata Gyokusho. Public Domain Image

Hope you enjoy this 390-word skip!
